GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Netflix's newest show proves the ultimate in summer fashion inspiration", "Netflix's Regency drama calls on a cocktail of lurid florals, ornate finery and romance.", "What we watch in our spare time has a habit of permeating our wardrobes; seamlessly filling a void we didn't know existed until Netflix's wardrobe department alerted us to the glaring holes. And we expect the streaming platform's new period drama Bridgerton to be no different. Based on the bestselling novels by Julia Quinn, Bridgerton, which comes courtesy of Shondaland via Netflix, takes audiences into the competitive world of Regency-era England where love and scandal appear to go hand-in-hand and the finery is just as you would expect: frilly, frothy and elaborate. The eight part series tracks the love lives of London's elite while the watchful, anonymous and acid-tongued Lady Whistledown (voiced by Julie Andrews) serves up gossip in her 'Society Papers'. Of course good, captivating drama requires costuming to match, especially a period piece under the eye of Rhimes. Regency England proved ripe for reimaging and Ellen Mirojnick, the woman responsible for the ostentatious oeuvre that reportedly includes some 7,500 pieces and took five months to perfect, approached the job with aplomb that doesn't disappoint. While traditional dress from the era might include soft-hued muslin dresses, Bridgerton has amped up the colour; a spectrum of vivid, sometimes-verging-on-garish florals, frosted gowns and all the tulle, organza and lace one might expect from a vampy period drama. Bonnets were also done away with, instead plaits and ringlets are layered with flowers, feathers and jewels; the latter demanding much screen time. Of course, modern wardrobes certainly don't require as much flair or froth but that's not to say one can't be inspired by the wallpaper florals, pastels in every flavour and jewel-encrusted chokers. And even if an empire line isn't quite your thing, the puffed-up cap sleeves and saccharine adornments might offer food for thought where your sartorial choices this summer are concerned. For those who can't get their head around the softly-softly approach of cottagecore, the Bridgerton aesthetic may be just what you're missing: sweetheart necklines, lace and the odd bow for good measure being key to the look; a Sofia Coppola meets Marie Antoinette take on history rather than BBC-approved Jane Austen. Mirojnick's contemporary approach, which saw the costume designer look to Dior for inspiration, also puts a lot more skin on show than would have been during the short-lived Regency period of the early 1800s. Scooped necklines and sheer sleeves are easy wins should you be inspired. All you need to conjure the Bridgerton mood is a velvet choker or layers of pearls to complete the look. The big takeaway for summer? No restraint. Bridgerton doesn't do romance in half measures, whether that's in its storylines or regalia. And if taking cues from Mirojnick's costumes be sure to lean into the charm of Bridgerton without getting lost in the theatrics. Temper your florals with silky blacks, or tone down the lace by opting into a reserved shade rather than Bridgerton bright."
